title: Joel
author: Joel son of Pethuel
date: Uncertain — either pre-exilic (~835–800 BC) or post-exilic (~400 BC); the book's reference to the day of the LORD and lack of mention of Assyria/Babylon suggest an early date to many scholars
setting: A catastrophic locust plague serves as the occasion for Joel's prophecy — a sign of the approaching Day of the LORD. The book calls the nation to lament and repentance.
purpose: To call all of Judah to genuine repentance in the face of divine judgment, and to promise that upon repentance God will restore and pour out his Spirit on all flesh — a promise Peter declares fulfilled at Pentecost (Acts 2).
themes: The Day of the LORD — judgment and salvation
The locust plague as divine warning
Repentance: 'return to me with all your heart'
The outpouring of the Spirit on all flesh (2:28-32)
Restoration: God will repay what the locusts devoured
Judgment on the nations in the Valley of Decision

outline.label: The Locust Plague and Call to Lament
The Day of the LORD — Spirit and Judgment

key_people.name: Joel ben Pethuel
key_people.role: Prophet of uncertain date, perhaps post-exilic; beyond his name and father's name nothing is known of him — the book focuses entirely on his message rather than his biography

context: Joel's date is disputed, ranging from the 9th to the 4th century BC; most contemporary scholars favour a post-exilic date (c. 400 BC) based on its place in the canon and certain literary features. The book responds to a catastrophic locust plague and drought that Joel interprets as a harbinger of 'the Day of the LORD' — a concept he develops more fully than any other OT book. The locust army of chapters 1–2 serves as both a literal disaster and a symbol of the eschatological army of judgment.
christ_connection: Peter's sermon on Pentecost (Acts 2:16–21) cites Joel 2:28–32 as the interpretive key to what the disciples' Spirit-filled speech means: the outpouring of the Spirit on all flesh, fulfilling Joel's promise, has begun in Jesus' resurrection and ascension. This is the NT's most explicit Joel-fulfilment: the 'last days' that Joel envisioned have been inaugurated by Christ. The promise that 'everyone who calls on the name of the LORD shall be saved' (Joel 2:32), applied to Jesus in Romans 10:13, establishes Jesus as YHWH's authorized representative to whom the Spirit-filled community now turns.

key_verses.note: 'I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy' — fulfilled at Pentecost (Acts 2:17) and marking the democratization of prophetic gifting in the new covenant age
'Everyone who calls on the name of the LORD shall be saved' — quoted in Romans 10:13 and applied to calling on Jesus, establishing the continuity between YHWH's salvation and Christ's
'Return to the LORD your God, for he is gracious and merciful, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love' — the theological foundation of Joel's call to repentance

themes_detail.title: The Day of the LORD as Crisis and Hope
themes_detail.text: Joel uses the phrase 'the Day of the LORD' more than any other prophet, and he consistently holds together its two dimensions: it is both terrible (a day of darkness and gloom, of armies and devastation) and glorious (a day of salvation for those who call on the LORD's name). The locust plague is both a real disaster and a sign — it shows what the Day of the LORD looks like in miniature, urging Israel to repent before the full version arrives. NT eschatology inherits this dual structure: Christ's return is both judgment and vindication.

themes_detail.title: Lament and Liturgy as the Pathway Through Crisis
themes_detail.text: Joel's response to disaster is not stoic endurance but structured liturgical lament: fast, call a solemn assembly, weep, rend your hearts rather than your garments (2:12–17). The community is to gather at the temple and cry out to God together. This is not crisis management but theology: disasters are occasions for the community to reorient its life toward God rather than scrambling for human solutions. The lament is performed with the assurance that God 'is gracious and merciful' (2:13) — the lament is possible precisely because of who God is.

themes_detail.title: The Outpouring of the Spirit on All Flesh
themes_detail.text: Joel 2:28–32 is the OT's most democratically radical promise about the Spirit: 'all flesh' will receive prophetic gifting — sons and daughters, old and young, male and female servants. This was unprecedented in a world where prophetic gifting was restricted to a relatively small number of designated individuals. Peter's citation of this passage at Pentecost (Acts 2) identifies the new covenant as the era in which Joel's vision is realized: every member of the community has direct access to God through his Spirit, and the body of Christ as a whole is a prophetic community.

themes_detail.title: Material Restoration as the Sign of Covenant Blessing
themes_detail.text: Joel's promise of restoration (2:18–27; 3:18) is earthy: grain, wine, and oil are restored; the years the locust has eaten are repaid; the threshing floors are full. This is not a spiritualization but a reaffirmation that God's covenant blessings include the material creation — that the grain and the rain are signs of his presence with his people. The eschatological vision of chapter 3 (mountains dripping with sweet wine, the hills flowing with milk, Judah inhabited forever) extends this material hope to its ultimate horizon: the full renewal of creation under God's reign.
